title: Defining Discernment
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Defining Discernment - Hebrews 5_14 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Review: What the Series Has Established So Far
subpoints: Lack of discernment is a sign of spiritual immaturity, a backsliding faith, or spiritual death
Three influences hinder discernment: internal (sinful nature), spiritual (Satan's armies), and cultural (secular worldview)
A low view of Scripture, theology, and God all prevent the development of biblical discernment

outline.title: A Working Definition of Discernment
subpoints: Discernment is the gained skill of understanding and applying God's Word, with the purpose of separating truth from error and right from wrong, and living according to what is right
It is a gained or acquired skill -- not inherited like breathing, but practiced like reading or public speaking
Wisdom is the application of the fear of God to life; discernment is wisdom applied to real situations
No one except Christ has mastered discernment completely; there is always room to grow

outline.title: The Biblical Vocabulary of Discernment
subpoints: The Hebrew root 'bin' means to define the space between things -- to separate good from evil, truth from error
1 Kings 3:9: Solomon asks for an understanding heart 'to discern between good and evil'
The Greek root 'krino/anakrino' means to judge, to make distinctions -- discernment is a kind of judging
1 Corinthians 2:14-15: the spiritual person judges all things, while the natural person cannot receive spiritual things

outline.title: Discernment as Understanding and Applying God's Word
subpoints: Discernment is built upon a right understanding of God and his ways as revealed in Scripture
Truth from error refers to doctrinal matters; right from wrong refers to ethical matters -- both are in view
You cannot be discerning while remaining ignorant of the Bible -- the two are inseparable
The Y-diagram: every decision flows from the heart, and discerning people practice making God-honoring decisions until it becomes more natural

outline.title: Key Principles About Discernment
subpoints: Discernment is a gift from God given to anyone who asks and pursues it (James 1:5)
Biblical discernment is only available to those indwelt by the Holy Spirit (1 Corinthians 2:14)
Discernment engages both heart and mind together -- it is not made in subjective feelings apart from reason
Discernment will not always be needed: in the new creation there will be no evil left to discern

summary:
In this third lesson of the Discernment series, Pastor Tito Lyro focuses on giving the class a precise, working definition of discernment and then examining each component of that definition carefully. The session begins with a brief review: previous lessons established that the absence of discernment signals spiritual immaturity, a backsliding faith, or even spiritual death, and identified three categories of influences that hinder it -- internal (the sinful nature), spiritual (Satan and his armies), and cultural (a secular worldview and a low view of Scripture and theology).

The definition Lyro adopts (adapted from Tim Challies) is: discernment is the gained skill of understanding and applying God's Word, with the purpose of separating truth from error and right from wrong, and living according to what is right. He unpacks each phrase. "Gained skill" signals that discernment is acquired through practice, like reading or public speaking, not inherited like breathing. "Applying" means discernment is wisdom in action -- wisdom can be merely theoretical, but discernment is always lived out. "God's Word" is central: discernment cannot be separated from the Bible; an ignorant or Bible-neglecting believer is not a discerning one.

A substantial portion of the lesson surveys the biblical vocabulary of discernment. In Hebrew, the root bin (meaning to define the space between things) underlies more than 250 occurrences where discernment, understanding, and separating truth from error are in view. In Greek, the krino/anakrino family means to judge and make distinctions -- so discernment is, in the scriptural sense, a form of judging. The class also distinguishes between doctrinal discernment (truth vs. error) and ethical discernment (right vs. wrong), noting that both require the same underlying skill of biblical analysis.

The lesson closes with five additional principles: discernment is a God-given gift available to all who ask; it is accessible only to those indwelt by the Holy Spirit; it always engages both the heart and the mind together; its ultimate proof is obedience; and it will not be needed in the new creation, which gives urgency to pursuing it now.
transcript:
## Introduction and Review

We will continue our series on discernment. I wanted to just share a little bit about what is happening on Sunday. We started a new series for Sunday morning service -- we are going to do the same series for Sunday morning and Sunday afternoon -- going through Psalm 119. There will be an introductory sermon this Sunday, and then we will go through the 22 stanzas of that Psalm. And to go along with that, at least for the month, in Sunday school we are going to consider two chapters in the confession to accompany the sermons on Psalm 119.

All right. We are talking about discernment. The Christian is called to be a discerning person -- not just some, but all of us are called to be discerning people. As we continue to look at the Scriptures to learn how we can become discerning people, it is important that we have a definition of discernment that is both mental and clear.

We want to have a definition that is clear. The Bible does not try to complicate things. There is no virtue in complicating things. One commentator tells a story about a lady who walked out of a service in the 1940s when someone who moved her asked her about what she had gotten from the sermon. She said, "I do not know what it is, it is going to work, it is beautiful" -- because that is not the Bible. We care because we want to understand it.

So as we talk about discernment, we want to make sure we have a definition that is both middle-ground and clear -- because if we want to move together toward a common goal, you need to be able to talk about what that goal is. Our common goal here is growing in discernment.

So let me introduce a definition that I think is both manageable and clear, provided by Tim Challies -- and I added a couple of things to make it a little clearer.

But before I go there, I want to remind us of what we have studied in the two previous lessons on discernment.

## Review of Previous Lessons

First time we saw that the lack of discernment is a sign of at least three things: spiritual immaturity, a backsliding faith (a faith that is moving backwards instead of forwards), and spiritual death. A lack of discernment is a danger sign.

Last week, we saw that there are three influences that make discernment difficult. There are challenges to discernment. The internal influences: the leftovers of our sinful nature getting in the way of our developing discernment. Spiritual influences: Satan and his armies getting in the way of our developing spiritual discernment. And then cultural influences as well.

Among the cultural influences: a secular worldview, a worldview not informed by the Scriptures, getting in the way of our developing spiritual discernment. A low view of Scripture, a low view of theology, a low view of God -- all of these cultural influences keep us from developing the spiritual discernment we need to live by.

Any questions on this brief review?

## The Definition of Discernment

All right. So here is a definition we are going to use for the rest of our time in the series on discernment. What is in brackets is the little parts that I added to Tim Challies' definition.

**Discernment is the gained skill of understanding and applying God's Word, with the purpose of separating truth from error and right from wrong, and living according to what is right.**

That is what discernment is. We are going to walk through this definition for the rest of our time and explain each part as we go. But before we do that -- does anything strike you as odd, or unclear, or wrong, as you see it?

*[Class discussion about the phrase "living according to what is right" -- that discernment without obedience falls short.]*

Yes. Solomon did not do what the last phrase demands -- he did not live according to what he discerned. He eventually fell into cultural influences that kept him from developing discernment even further. When he looked at the world with a separate perspective and said "all the kings multiply," he could not resist the same. There is that.

And you may be applying and living according to this for a season, and may not for another season, and come back to it as well. The definition does not solve it all -- I do not think God immediately gave Solomon every possible discernment, and Solomon also had to work at it. Growth in discernment is not perfect from the start. Anything else?

## Discernment as a Gained Skill

The first component of the definition: discernment is a **gained** skill -- two words for gained: acquired. This is important because discernment is not an inherent ability like breathing or chewing. We need to learn discernment. It is more like reading or public speaking, which must be practiced and must be improved. That is why I call it a gained or acquired skill.

There is no person who has obtained a level of expertise that allows him or her to leave growth in discernment behind. Besides Jesus Christ -- who is in heaven and we are waiting for him to return -- there is not one person who can say, "I have mastered discernment completely and can move on to something else."

The more we know biblical truth, the more our ability to discern will increase. This is true of the Christian walk in general -- the more we practice what God calls us to do, the more we are able to do it.

I use the Y-diagram -- you may have seen this in counseling. Every decision comes from the heart. Proverbs 4:23 says that from the heart flow the springs of life. As a person thinks in his or her heart, so is he or she. We get to the crucial point of making decisions. And it is our thinking that is going to draw on how we make that decision. The decisions that are with the flesh are easy to see -- those are easiest to make, whatever we are more used to doing. To choose righteousness might be difficult at first, but the more we practice it, the easier it becomes.

Remember Matthew 11:28-30: "Come to me, all you who are weary and burdened, and I will give you rest. My yoke is easy and my burden is light." The more we get used to making decisions against the flesh, the easier those decisions become. And the more we go the way of the flesh, the harder life becomes -- Proverbs 13:15: "The way of the transgressor is hard." So the more we get used to making decisions against the flesh, the easier it gets. The more discernment increases.

## Understanding and Applying God's Word

Discernment is the gained skill of **understanding** -- it depends upon a right understanding of God and his ways. It would be helpful to look at the biblical vocabulary used to talk about discerning and understanding, and how these two things really work together.

In our translation, the New King James Version, the word "discernment" is used only four times. In the ESV, it is used seven times. But if you look at the original language, Hebrew and Greek, there are over 250 occurrences of words related to discernment.

In Hebrew, the big root word -- the **bin** root -- often has to do with defining the space between things. If you gave your balance between, guess what else you had to figure out? What is over here and what is over there. To separate, to figure out the space between things. That is the idea generally translated as understanding or discernment. And as we see discernment, this word is used with words having to do with judging, knowing, or teaching where a person must distinguish between multiple options.

For example, 1 Kings 3:9, the passage about Solomon: he asks God for wisdom, saying, "Therefore give to your servant an understanding heart to judge your people, that I may discern between good and evil." It is interesting that the same root is used twice -- the ability to discern between good and evil, to separate them, to see the space between them, to know where they belong.

The servant is thinking in black-and-white terms -- drawing clear lines between what is truth and error, between what is good and evil. And we are going to see that there is a difference between separating truth from error (doctrinal) and separating right from wrong (ethical). They are different categories, though they are related.

In Greek, there is a group of words built on the **krino** root -- meaning to make distinction, to judge. In Scripture, discernment is judging. That is a multicultural problem in our world, where supposedly we are not to judge anybody. But discernment in Scripture is precisely the process of judging.

1 Corinthians 2:14-15: "But the natural man does not receive the things of the Spirit of God, for they are foolishness to him; nor can he know them, because they are spiritually discerned. But he who is spiritual judges all things, yet he himself is rightly judged by no one." All three bolded words there -- discerned, judges, judged -- are the same root. Part of discernment is being able to judge.

## Wisdom vs. Discernment

We can think of wisdom as the application of the fear of God to life. It is living in such a way that we place God above all else. Psalm 111:10: "The fear of the Lord is the beginning of wisdom, and the knowledge of the Holy One is understanding." Wisdom is the skill of living a God-honoring life.

Discernment is simply wisdom applied to real life situations. Wisdom can be known without being lived out; discernment is always lived out. That is a little bit of a difference between them. Discernment is wisdom in action.

## Applying God's Word

Discernment is the gained skill of understanding and **applying** God's Word. Discernment is wisdom in action, the seeking to apply the skill we have been practicing.

When you are on a sports team and you practice and practice, the goal is to get that skill so refined that when game time comes, you execute it without issue. That is the difference between wisdom and discernment -- discernment actually does it.

And **God's Word is central to this.** If God is going to reveal himself, he does so through the Bible. Not through visions, not through dreams, not through new prophets, not through new revelation. But through the Bible -- and primarily through this special revelation. To be discerning, we must first be students of the Bible. You cannot be discerning and not be a student of the Bible -- you cannot separate those two things.

Psalm 119:104: "Through your precepts I get understanding; therefore I hate every false way." It is through the precepts of God, the Word of God, that we gain discernment. Discernment and the Bible cannot be separated.

## Separating Truth from Error and Right from Wrong

So discernment is the gained skill of understanding and applying God's Word, with the **purpose of separating.** We are trying to do something here -- the word "separating" means bringing things apart, distinguishing them.

Think of a laser level versus a physical level. A laser level shows with perfect clarity any deviation from what is straight. The Bible teaches what is true, leaving what is false in clear relief. There are very few things in the Bible that are not clear. There are very few things where it is very difficult to separate because the Bible clearly separates evil from good, truth from error.

When we talk about **truth from error**, we speak of doctrine and theology. The Shorter Catechism question 3 asks: "What do the Scriptures principally teach?" The answer: "The Scriptures principally teach what man is to believe concerning God, and what duty God requires of man." So if you put everything the Bible teaches into just two drawers -- one drawer is what the Bible teaches us to believe, the other is what it requires us to do -- very few passages fall outside those two drawers. So the Scripture teaches us to discern: separating truth from error refers to doctrine; right from wrong refers to ethics. There is a difference between those two, though they often overlap.

## Key Principles About Discernment

Let me go beyond the definition for just a moment and make some statements.

**First:** Spiritual discernment is a gift from God given to anyone who asks for it and pursues it. James says if anyone lacks wisdom, let him ask God -- the Father who gives generously and without reproach. But at the same time, you need to work at it. So it is a gift from God, available to anyone who asks and pursues it.

**Second:** Biblical discernment is only available to those who are indwelt by the Holy Spirit. We already saw this in 1 Corinthians 2:14 -- only people who have the Spirit can discern the things of God. A non-Christian cannot have true biblical discernment. Sometimes they get things right, like a broken clock that is right twice a day -- but that is not true biblical discernment.

**Third:** Discernment involves the heart and the head since they are the same thing. The Bible does not make a distinction between the heart and the mind the way Western culture does. They are the same organ. Discernment is not made in subjective feelings apart from reason. Spiritual discernment is a pursuit that must always engage the mind.

**Fourth:** The purpose of discernment is to help us glorify God and enjoy him. That is why we pursue it -- because we want to glorify God, and then we want to enjoy him.

**Fifth:** Discernment will not always be needed. Discernment is a skill suited to a world where there is evil. When Christ returns and makes all things new, there will be no need for discernment because there will be no evil. So there is something we pursue really hard now in hopes of not needing it later.

## Closing

We must be willing to make clear statements about what is right and what is wrong. We must be willing to think deeply about issues and dedicate time and effort to learning what is right and what is true. We must also be willing to grow in our knowledge of God and the Bible, because that is where we learn God's way. And discernment is trying to figure out God's way and follow it. Remember the last part of the definition: "and living according to what is right." If we do all these things and just do not live according to what is right, then we are really not practicing biblical discernment.

Any last questions or comments? Let us pray.

*Father, you speak through your Word. Help us to experience the truth of it in both our hearts and minds, to have our eyes and ears open to hear it, and to help us to live according to what is right. In Christ's name. Amen.*
